name: Veuve Clicquot Yellow Label Brut
description: Yellow Label is the non-vintage brut that carries the house of Veuve Clicquot — instantly identifiable by the saturated orange-yellow livery trademarked in 1877 and among the best-selling champagnes in the world. The style is deliberately pinot noir-led: fuller, toastier and more vinous than many NV bruts, softened by a high proportion of reserve wines that iron out vintage differences. Its scale is industrial, its consistency the point — the same biscuity apple-and-brioche profile in Tokyo, Chicago or Reims. Behind the ubiquity stands one of Champagne's most consequential histories: the widow Clicquot's inventions in the 1810s helped turn cloudy, sweet fizz into the clear, exportable luxury the region sells today, and her house's NV brut remains the reference point for celebration champagne.
history: Philippe Clicquot founded the house in 1772; his son François died in 1805, leaving 27-year-old widow ('veuve') Barbe-Nicole Clicquot-Ponsardin in charge — one of the first women to run a champagne house. She invented the riddling table around 1816, solving champagne's clarity problem, ran the 1811 'comet vintage' past wartime blockades to Russia in 1814, and produced an early blended rosé in 1818. The house joined LVMH in 1987; the distinctive yellow label was registered in 1877.
production: Pinot noir leads the blend at roughly half, with meunier and chardonnay completing it, sourced from a broad network including the house's own largely grand- and premier-cru vineyards. Reserve wines — often a third or more of the blend, some aged many years — provide the house signature. After tank fermentation and blending, secondary fermentation happens in bottle with about three years on lees (double the legal minimum), then disgorgement with a dosage near 9–10 g/L.
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: Baked apple, pear and mirabelle with brioche, vanilla pod and a toasty, biscuity autolysis note
tastingNotes.palate: Round and vinous for an NV brut; orchard fruit and citrus over creamy mousse and gingerbread spice
tastingNotes.finish: Medium-long, toasty and dry with fresh acidity

facts: Madame Clicquot's riddling table (c. 1816) — allegedly adapted from her kitchen table — let sediment be worked into the neck of the bottle, making clear champagne commercially possible.
In 1814 the widow smuggled 10,000+ bottles of the 1811 'comet vintage' through wartime blockades to Russia, locking in the market that made her fortune.
The trademark yellow-orange label, registered in 1877, was originally a way to mark the house's dry champagne for the British market.

timeline.event: Philippe Clicquot founds the house in Reims
Madame Clicquot devises the riddling table, revolutionizing clarification

name: Krug Grande Cuvée
description: Krug Grande Cuvée inverts champagne's usual hierarchy: rather than reserving its best wine for vintage years, the house pours its greatest effort into a multi-vintage blend recreated from scratch each year. A typical édition assembles well over a hundred base wines from ten or more different years, drawn from a library of reserve wines that can stretch back a decade and a half. First fermentation in small, seasoned 205-litre oak casks and six-plus years on the lees give the wine its signature breadth — honeyed brioche, dried fruit, hazelnut and citrus in layered procession. Each release is numbered (the édition system began publicly with the 163ème) and traceable via the Krug iD code on the back label. It is champagne argued as gastronomy, and priced accordingly.
history: Joseph Krug, a German-born former Jacquesson employee, founded his house in Reims in 1843 around a heretical idea recorded in his cherry-red notebook: the fullest expression of champagne should be independent of any single year's weather. His multi-vintage 'Private Cuvée' embodied the idea for over a century; in the late 1970s the house relaunched it as Grande Cuvée. Krug joined LVMH in 1999, with the founding family's sixth generation still involved and Julie Cavil named cellar master in 2020.
production: Plots across Champagne — including the walled Clos du Mesnil and Clos d'Ambonnay used for other cuvées — are vinified parcel by parcel in small 205-litre used oak casks, kept apart through primary fermentation. The blend unites pinot noir, chardonnay and meunier: typically 120+ wines from at least ten years, with reserve wines up to fifteen years old. After bottling, secondary fermentation and a minimum of about six to seven years on lees precede disgorgement and a restrained dosage.
barrel: First fermentation in small 205-litre seasoned oak casks; no new-oak flavor sought
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after extended lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: Honeyed brioche, hazelnut and almond nougat with dried apricot, candied citrus, gingerbread and blossom
tastingNotes.palate: Full, vinous and layered; baked apple, lemon curd and marzipan carried on a fine, creamy mousse
tastingNotes.finish: Exceptionally long — toasted nuts, honey and citrus oil unwinding slowly

servingTemp: 9–12 °C in a tulip or white-wine glass — the house discourages narrow flutes
facts: Every bottle carries a Krug iD code revealing the édition's composition and disgorgement details.
A single Grande Cuvée édition typically blends more than 120 wines from at least ten different years.
Founder Joseph Krug's 1848 cherry-red notebook setting out his blending philosophy still guides the house — and is still in its possession.

timeline.event: Joseph Krug founds the house in Reims
Grande Cuvée succeeds the Private Cuvée as the house's flagship
Krug iD introduced, numbering éditions and dating every bottle

name: Freixenet Cordon Negro Brut Cava
description: Cordon Negro — the frosted 'black bottle' — is the cava that introduced much of the world to Spanish sparkling wine. Made by Freixenet in Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, the capital of the cava industry, it uses the same bottle-fermentation method as champagne but with Catalonia's native trio of macabeo, xarel·lo and parellada, yielding a lighter, apple-and-citrus style with an earthy, faintly smoky Mediterranean accent. Launched in 1974 and propelled by decades of memorable advertising, it became one of the best-selling traditional-method sparkling wines on the planet, delivering genuine secondary-fermentation character — fine bubbles, a touch of pastry — at an everyday price. It is the standing rebuttal to the idea that bottle-fermented fizz must be expensive.
history: The Ferrer and Sala families, growers in the Penedès since the 1800s, sold their first sparkling wine under the Freixenet name in 1914 — the brand taken from La Freixeneda, the family's ash-grove estate. Carta Nevada's frosted bottle arrived in 1941, and Cordon Negro's matte black version in 1974 became a global icon. Germany's Henkell took majority control in 2018, creating the world's largest sparkling-wine group.
production: Macabeo, xarel·lo and parellada from the Penedès are pressed and fermented separately in stainless steel, then blended and bottled for a second fermentation in the bottle, as cava's DO rules require. The wine rests on its lees underground in Sant Sadurní d'Anoia for well beyond the nine-month legal minimum before riddling, disgorgement and a brut dosage, preserving crisp green-apple fruit with light toasty notes.
filtration: Riddled and disgorged after lees aging

tastingNotes.nose: Green apple, pear and lemon with white flowers, a little bread dough and a dry earthy note
tastingNotes.palate: Crisp and light-bodied; citrus and orchard fruit with brisk bubbles and a hint of almond
tastingNotes.finish: Short-to-medium, clean and dry with a gently bitter apple-skin close

facts: Cava must be made by the traditional method — second fermentation in the bottle — just like champagne; the difference is place, grapes and price.
Freixenet means 'ash grove' in Catalan, after the family farm La Freixeneda.
Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, Freixenet's hometown, produces the overwhelming majority of all cava; the company's cellars tunnel directly beneath the town.

timeline.event: First sparkling wines sold under the Freixenet name
Cordon Negro launched in its matte black bottle
Henkell acquires majority control of Freixenet

name: La Marca Prosecco
description: La Marca, in its powder-blue livery, is one of the best-selling proseccos in the United States and a fair archetype of the modern category: glera grapes from the plains and hills of Treviso, fermented to sparkle in pressurized tanks rather than bottles, and released young to catch the grape's pear-blossom aromatics. Where champagne builds flavor from yeast and time, prosecco aims for immediacy — green apple, honeydew, white peach and flowers on a soft, gently off-dry palate with easygoing bubbles. Made by a consortium of Trevigiano growers' cooperatives and marketed in America by E. & J. Gallo, it fueled prosecco's explosive 2010s growth and remains the default pour for spritzes and casual celebration.
history: La Marca was founded in 1968 in Oderzo as a union of winegrowing cooperatives in the province of Treviso — 'La Marca Trevigiana' being the historic name for the territory. As prosecco's DOC framework matured (the modern Prosecco DOC dates to 2009, when the grape was officially renamed glera), the consortium's export brand rode the global boom, becoming a top-selling import prosecco in the US through its partnership with E. & J. Gallo.
production: Glera grapes from member growers across the Prosecco DOC zone in Treviso are pressed and cold-fermented in stainless steel. Secondary fermentation follows the Charmat (Martinotti) method: the base wine re-ferments in sealed, pressurized tanks for several weeks, preserving primary fruit and floral aromatics, then is filtered and bottled under pressure with extra-dry sweetness of roughly 12–17 g/L.
filtration: Sterile filtered and bottled under pressure

tastingNotes.nose: Pear, green apple and honeydew with acacia blossom and a hint of white peach
tastingNotes.palate: Soft, lightly off-dry and fresh; juicy orchard fruit with a gentle, frothy mousse
tastingNotes.finish: Short, clean and fruity — made for refreshment, not contemplation

servingTemp: 6–8 °C, well chilled; drink the youngest bottle you can find
facts: Prosecco's grape was officially renamed 'glera' in 2009 so 'Prosecco' could be protected as a place name.
The Charmat tank method costs far less than bottle fermentation and deliberately preserves fresh fruit aromas over bready autolysis.
La Marca is a consortium brand pooling thousands of grower families across Treviso's cooperatives.

timeline.event: La Marca founded in Oderzo as a union of Treviso cooperatives
Modern Prosecco DOC established; glera name adopted for the grape

name: Westvleteren 12
style: Trappist Quadrupel / Belgian Strong Dark Ale
description: Westvleteren 12 is the most mythologized beer on earth: a 10.2% Trappist quadrupel brewed by the monks of Saint-Sixtus Abbey in the West Flanders countryside and sold, in principle, only at the abbey gate by advance reservation. The bottles carry no label — all legal text lives on the crown cap (gold for the 12) — and production is capped at what the small community needs to sustain itself. Years of number-one rankings on beer-rating sites turned a modest monastic economy into a pilgrimage phenomenon, with gate-price cases resold abroad at a tenfold markup. The beer itself justifies much of the noise: dark fig-and-raisin fruit, bready caramel, peppery yeast spice and a port-like depth that keeps evolving for years in the cellar, all from an unfiltered, bottle-conditioned brew of monastic simplicity.
history: The abbey of Saint-Sixtus was founded in 1831 and began brewing in 1838. Unlike other Trappist houses, the monks refused commercial expansion: since 1946 sales have been direct from the monastery, and brewing capacity has barely grown. Westvleteren 12 first appeared in 1940. A one-time exception came in 2012, when gift packs were sold internationally to fund urgent abbey renovations. The monks' stated position remains that they brew to live, not live to brew.
production: A classic quadrupel grist of pale malts deepened with dark candi sugar, hopped modestly with continental varieties for balance rather than bitterness. Fermentation uses yeast obtained from Westmalle, the strain contributing dark-fruit esters and warming phenolic spice. The beer is neither filtered nor pasteurized and referments in the bottle, gaining complexity for years. The monks brew only around 60 weeks' worth of batches annually, assisted by a few lay workers, inside the abbey walls.
coloring: No added coloring — color from dark malts and candi sugar

tastingNotes.nose: Raisin, fig and plum bread with dark caramel, clove and a winey, port-like sweetness
tastingNotes.palate: Full and velvety; dark fruit compote, toffee and brown sugar cut by peppery yeast spice and soft carbonation
tastingNotes.finish: Long and warming — dried fruit, cocoa and spice drying slowly to a bittersweet close

servingTemp: 12–14 °C, poured gently to leave the yeast in the bottle; cellars beautifully for 5+ years
facts: Bottles are sold unlabeled — all legally required information is printed on the crown cap.
Buying at the abbey requires an advance reservation and comes with a per-car limit and a polite request not to resell.
The 2012 international gift-pack sale — a one-off to pay for abbey renovations — was the only sanctioned wide release in the beer's history.
Gate price is a few euros a bottle; secondary-market prices abroad routinely reach $20–25.

timeline.event: Monks restrict sales to the abbey itself, ending commercial distribution
One-time worldwide gift-pack sale funds abbey renovations
